title: Tactical Foundations — The CCT System
subtitle: "The winner of the game is the player who makes the next-to-last mistake." — Savielly Tartakower

sections.title: 3.1 Why Tactics Win Games at This Level

blocks.html:
Between ELO 400-600, games are decided by tactics — not strategy, not positional understanding, not opening theory. The player who spots one more tactic than their opponent wins.

The hard truth: 80% of games are decided by:

  1. One player hangs a piece
  2. One player misses a simple tactic
  3. One player doesn't see a checkmate in 1-2 moves

Your job is to stop being the player who misses these.

CCT stands for Checks, Captures, Threats. It's a systematic way to analyze any position. Run it on every single turn.

The CCT Scan (For YOUR Move)

  1. CHECKS: "Can I check my opponent's king?"
  2. CAPTURES: "What can I capture? In what order?"
  3. THREATS: "What threats can I create?"

Why this order? Checks are the most forcing — opponent MUST respond. Captures are next. Threats are third.

The CCT Scan (For THEIR Move)

After your opponent moves, run the scan from their perspective. If their move looks suspicious, there's probably a tactic.

A check must be answered. Three ways to escape: move the king, block the check, capture the checking piece.

Check With a Purpose

TypePurpose
Driving checkForce king to worse square
Discovery checkUncover attack while checking
Double checkCheck from 2 pieces — king MUST move!
CheckmateEnd the game

The Power of Double Check

A double check is the most powerful move in chess. The king MUST move — you cannot block or capture two checking pieces at once.

blocks.caption: Nxg6+! — DOUBLE CHECK. The knight checks from g6, AND the bishop on c4 discovers check. The Black king MUST move. After ...Kf8 or ...Ke7, White has Nxh8 winning the rook.

blocks.html:
The Capture Hierarchy

When multiple captures are possible:

  1. Pawns capture first (cheapest, 1 point)
  2. Minor pieces next (knights, bishops = 3)
  3. Rooks next (5)
  4. Queen last (9)

Always capture with the least valuable piece first.

Removal of the Defender

Sometimes you can't capture the piece you want because it's defended. Solution: capture the defender first.

blocks.caption: Bcf1-g5! — Pinning the defender. Black's knight on f6 defends the d5 pawn. The bishop pins it to the queen. Now White can win the d5 pawn.

blocks.html:
Desperado

When one of your pieces is going to be captured, make sure it takes something with it. A desperado piece "sells its life dearly" by taking the most valuable enemy piece it can before being captured.

Types of Threats

ThreatDifficulty to Spot
ForkEasy
PinMedium
SkewerMedium
Discovered attackHard
ZwischenzugHard

Zwischenzug (The In-Between Move)

Your opponent expects you to recapture immediately. Instead, you throw in a check or threat first, then recapture.

sections.title: 3.6 The Blunder Check — Most Important Habit

blocks.html:
Every time you're about to make a move, do a blunder check:

  1. What will my opponent's best reply be?
  2. Am I moving a piece to an attacked square?
  3. Am I leaving something undefended?

This takes 2-3 seconds and will save more pieces than any other single habit.

Sit on your hands for 5 seconds after deciding on a move. Look at the board one more time. Many blunders happen because players move instantly.

Theme 1: The f7/f2 Square

The f7 (Black) and f2 (White) squares are the weakest at the start — only defended by the king!

blocks.caption: Scholar's Mate threat. White's queen and bishop both target f7, which is only defended by the Black king. Qxf7 would be checkmate! Black must play ...Qe7, ...Qf6, ...g6, or ...Nh6.

blocks.html:
Theme 2: Knight on the Rim

"Knight on the rim is dim." A knight on the edge controls half as many squares. Trap opponent knights on the a-file or h-file.

Theme 3: Overloaded Defender

A piece defending two things is "overloaded." Attack one, and it can't protect both.

Theme 4: Discovered Attack With Tempo

Moving a piece to reveal an attack, while the moving piece ALSO creates a threat. Two threats at once!

Training Method: The 30-Second CCT Scan

  1. Go to any chess puzzle position
  2. Set a timer for 30 seconds
  3. Count: "Checks from me: ___. Captures from me: ___. Threats from me: ___."
  4. Do the same for your opponent
  5. Only AFTER the full scan, start calculating

Do this 50 times. By puzzle 50, you'll do it in 10 seconds automatically.

Pro tip: The Chess99 Tactical Trainer enforces this — you must identify CCTs before solving puzzles. Complete the CCT phases every time!

checkpoint: Do you automatically scan for checks, captures, and threats on every move? Can you spot a fork, pin, skewer, and discovered attack? Do you do a blunder check before every move? If yes, proceed to Chapter 4.
summary:
SkillWhat You Must Master
CCT ScanChecks -> Captures -> Threats (every move, both sides)
Blunder Check5-second final scan before moving
Capture OrderLeast valuable piece first
Removal of DefenderCapture the defender, then the target
Tactical Themesf7/f2, overloaded defender, knight on rim, discovered attacks
CCT Practice50 puzzles minimum, 30-second scan each
